summary: Hotz argues that the fundamental problem with the state is not who controls it, but that coercive power over others exists at all. He contends that representative democracy tricks people into supporting state power by making them fear what the opposing side might do if they gain control. Rather than political voice or reform, he advocates for exit—using technology to make state functions obsolete by decentralizing basic needs like healthcare, transportation, and energy. The outcome depends on whether defensive or offensive technology prevails, with cryptography offering hope that defense can win.
key_points: The state's trick is making you support its power because you fear the other side's state power—you're trapped into participation by fear, not hope
Technology's fundamental nature (offensive vs defensive) will determine humanity's future—crypto suggests defense can win
The solution isn't limiting state power philosophically but making it irrelevant through decentralized technology that provides basic needs better and cheaper
Centralized tech like Waymo is dangerous because it creates revocable rights; comma.ai exists to keep self-driving decentralized
There's no such thing as 'centralization of power for good'—even well-intentioned systems will eventually be controlled by someone evil
money_quotes: The problem with the state is that is it bad, not that the wrong people are in it.
You no longer believe that things will get better, but you are forced to be involved because you fear things will get worse.
Instead of voice, you just leave.
I don't want to live in a world where P = NP.
If you can provide electricity, water, food, money, transportation, education, healthcare, and protection, you won't destroy the state, you'll disrupt it.
I didn't start comma because I think solving self driving cars is good for the world. I think the technology is inevitable, but whether it's centrally owned and controlled is not.
There's no such thing as 'centralization of power for good', even if you are good someone evil will eventually take the reins of whatever you built.
Don't be so stupid as to think you'll be one wearing the boot. You won't.
key_insight: The path to freedom isn't reforming or capturing state power, but making it irrelevant through decentralized technology that renders centralized control unnecessary.
